explain.rule: Countable nouns are things you can count: one book, two books, three books. They have a singular and a plural form. Uncountable nouns are things you cannot count individually: water, milk, information, advice, furniture. They have no plural form — you never say 'informations' or 'advices'. To talk about quantities, use some (positive statements and offers), any (negatives and questions), much/many, a lot of, a few/a little, enough.
explain.why_matters: Choosing the right quantifier (much vs many, a few vs a little) depends entirely on whether the noun is countable or uncountable. This affects almost every sentence where you talk about amounts or quantities.
explain.hebrew_note: In Hebrew, many nouns that are uncountable in English can be pluralised (like מידעים). In English, information, advice, furniture, luggage, money, news, hair, traffic, and many others are always singular and uncountable — never add -s. Also: 'news' is singular in English — 'The news is good' (not 'the news are good').
explain.tip: If you can put a number in front of it, it is countable (three books, five people). If you cannot (three informations? — NO), it is uncountable. For uncountable nouns, use 'a piece of' or 'a bit of' to talk about one unit: a piece of advice, a piece of furniture, a bit of information.

quantifiers.some: positive sentences: I have some money. | offers: Would you like some coffee?
quantifiers.any: negatives: I don't have any money. | questions: Do you have any questions?
quantifiers.much: uncountable in negatives/questions: I don't have much time. How much water?
quantifiers.many: countable in negatives/questions: I don't have many friends. How many people?
quantifiers.a_lot_of: both countable and uncountable in positives: I have a lot of time. I have a lot of books.
quantifiers.a_few: countable positives (small number): I have a few ideas.
quantifiers.a_little: uncountable positives (small amount): I have a little money.
quantifiers.few: countable — negative meaning (not many): Few people came = almost nobody
quantifiers.little: uncountable — negative meaning (not much): Little time remains = almost no time

good_examples.sentence: I need some information about the schedule.
good_examples.audio_text: I need some information about the schedule.
good_examples.note: information = uncountable, no plural, use some
questions = countable (plural) + any in a question

good_examples.sentence: She has a lot of experience.
good_examples.audio_text: She has a lot of experience.
good_examples.note: experience (as general skill) = uncountable → a lot of

good_examples.sentence: I only have a little time before my meeting.
good_examples.audio_text: I only have a little time before my meeting.

good_examples.sentence: Can I give you a piece of advice?
good_examples.audio_text: Can I give you a piece of advice?
good_examples.note: advice = uncountable → a piece of advice (not 'an advice')

good_examples.sentence: How many people are coming to the party?
good_examples.audio_text: How many people are coming to the party?

good_examples.sentence: We have a few options to consider.
good_examples.audio_text: We have a few options to consider.

good_examples.sentence: The news is very worrying today.
good_examples.audio_text: The news is very worrying today.
good_examples.note: news = uncountable, always singular — the news IS

bad_examples.wrong: Can you give me an advice?
bad_examples.correct: Can you give me some advice? / a piece of advice?
bad_examples.reason: Advice is uncountable — no 'an advice'. Use some advice or a piece of advice.
Money is uncountable → use much, not many. Many is for countable nouns.

bad_examples.wrong: She has a lot of furnitures.
bad_examples.correct: She has a lot of furniture.
bad_examples.reason: Furniture is uncountable — no plural. Never 'furnitures'.
News is uncountable and always takes a singular verb — the news IS.
few is for countable nouns. little is for uncountable nouns. Money = uncountable → little money.

exercises.question: How ___ (many/much) sugar do you take in your coffee?
exercises.explanation: Sugar is uncountable → how much sugar.
exercises.hebrew_note: סוכר = שם עצם שאינו ניתן לספירה → much

exercises.question: There are ___ (many/much) students in my English class.
exercises.explanation: Students is countable → many students.

exercises.question: I don't have ___ (much/many) time — I'm very busy.
exercises.explanation: Time is uncountable → much time (in a negative).

exercises.question: She gave me ___ (a/some) good advice.
exercises.hint: Advice = uncountable. Can you say 'an advice'?
exercises.explanation: Advice is uncountable — no 'an advice'. Use some advice.

exercises.question: There's ___ (little/few) milk left in the fridge.
exercises.explanation: Milk is uncountable → a little milk (small positive amount).

exercises.question: We have ___ (a few/a little) options to choose from.
exercises.explanation: Options is countable → a few options.

exercises.question: There was a lot of ___ (traffic/traffics) on the motorway.
exercises.explanation: Traffic is uncountable — never add -s. A lot of traffic.

exercises.question: Do you have ___ (some/any) questions for me?
exercises.hint: Question → do you need some or any?
exercises.explanation: Questions in this form → any. Any in questions and negatives.

exercises.question: I need ___ (some/any) information about visa requirements.
exercises.explanation: Positive statements use some: I need some information.

exercises.question: She doesn't have ___ (much/many) experience in this field.
exercises.explanation: Experience as a general skill is uncountable → much experience.

exercises.question: How ___ (much/many) people work in your company?
exercises.explanation: People is countable → how many people.

exercises.question: We bought ___ (a few/a little) new furniture for the living room.
exercises.explanation: Furniture is uncountable. Use some furniture or a piece of furniture, not a few.

exercises.question: Which sentence is correct?
exercises.explanation: Information is uncountable — no article 'an', no plural 's'.

exercises.question: Choose the correct quantifier: '___ people know about this.'
exercises.hint: Few (without 'a') has a negative meaning — almost nobody
exercises.explanation: Few people = almost nobody. A few people = some (positive meaning).

exercises.question: The news ___ not good today.
exercises.hint: Is 'news' singular or plural in English?
exercises.explanation: News is always singular in English — the news IS.

exercises.question: She has ___ experience in marketing.
exercises.explanation: A lot of works with uncountable nouns in positive statements.

exercises.question: Is there ___ petrol in the car?
exercises.explanation: Questions typically use any: Is there any petrol?

exercises.question: Which of these is uncountable?
exercises.hint: Which one cannot be counted individually?
exercises.explanation: Luggage is uncountable. Books, cars, and ideas are all countable.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'Can you give me an advice about my CV?'
exercises.answer: Can you give me some advice about my CV?
exercises.explanation: Advice is uncountable — no 'an advice'. Use some advice or a piece of advice.
exercises.hebrew_note: advice = שם עצם שאינו ניתן לספירה. לא 'an advice'

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'There isn't many milk left.'
exercises.explanation: Milk is uncountable → much. Many is for countable nouns.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'She bought three furnitures for her apartment.'
exercises.answer: She bought some furniture / three pieces of furniture for her apartment.
exercises.hint: Furniture = uncountable — can it be plural?
exercises.explanation: Furniture is uncountable — never 'furnitures'. Use some furniture or pieces of furniture.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'I have few money in my wallet.'
exercises.answer: I have little money in my wallet.
exercises.hint: few is for countable nouns. Money is uncountable.
exercises.explanation: Few = for countable. Little = for uncountable. Money = uncountable → little money.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.hint: How much for uncountable / price questions
exercises.explanation: How much = uncountable quantity or price.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: How many people are there waiting?
exercises.explanation: How many + countable plural noun.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: Can you give me a piece of advice?
exercises.hint: 'A piece of' is used with uncountable nouns to refer to one unit
exercises.explanation: a piece of advice = one item of advice. A piece of information, a piece of furniture.

speak.sentence: How much time do we have before the presentation starts?
chunks: How much time do we have
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Presentation' = prez-en-TAY-shun — four syllables, stress on third.

speak.sentence: I don't have much experience with this kind of software.
Can you give me some information about the opening hours?
chunks: Can you give me some information
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Information' = in-fer-MAY-shun — four syllables, stress on third. 'Opening' = OH-puh-ning.

speak.sentence: There are a few options but not much time to decide.
chunks: but not much time to decide.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Options' = OP-shunz. 'Decide' = dih-SYD — stress on second syllable.

speak.sentence: How many people work in your department?
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Department' = dih-PART-ment — three syllables, stress on second.

speak.sentence: I only have a little money left until the end of the month.
chunks: I only have a little money left
until the end of the month.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Until' = un-TIL — stress on second. 'Month' = MUNTH — the 'th' sound at the end.

speak.sentence: The news is full of bad stories at the moment.
chunks: The news is full of bad stories
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Stories' = STAWR-eez. 'Moment' = MOH-ment. Remember: the news IS (not are).

speak.sentence: Do you have any questions before we start?
